title: Tell me about a time when you prioritized the needs of a customer over your organization's needs.

transcript.text: Tell me about a time when you prioritized the needs of a customer over your organization's needs.

transcript.thinking:
The user is asking a behavioral question: "Tell me about a time when you prioritized the needs of a customer over your organization's needs."

This is a classic behavioral question that maps to Amazon's **Customer Obsession** Leadership Principle — "Leaders start with the customer and work backwards. They work vigorously to earn and keep customer trust. Although leaders pay attention to competitors, they obsess over customers."

Let me think about which story fits best here. The target company is Amazon, and the two LPs the recruiter named are **Learn and Be Curious** and **Bias for Action**. However, this question is clearly about **Customer Obsession**, so I should frame it that way.
I'm reviewing my story bank for examples where I made a choice that benefited the customer even if it meant deviating from what the organization might have expected. The DSD notifications story shows how I validated with actual users first and narrowed the scope to what was genuinely helpful rather than shipping a broader solution. The shared library story is another strong fit — suppliers needed self-serve debugging visibility, and even though the org was decommissioning the old tool, I built the audit pipeline to give them that capability they were asking for. so he reduced scope to only the actionable states. That's a clearer example of pushing back against org pressure to ship more. W3 also works — the senior engineer flagged that silent drops on a customer-facing audit trail were unacceptable, and Anshul advocated for fixing it despite the added complexity. The strongest tension might be W7 combined with the supplier self-service work, where customer needs (reducing notification fatigue, enabling faster debugging) genuinely conflicted with what the org initially wanted to ship. — that's the customer need right there. The org wanted to cut costs by decommissioning Splunk, and Anshul delivered on that, but he specifically built the BigQuery external tables piece to address what suppliers actually needed: self-serve debugging visibility. The org might've settled for a cheaper internal logging system, but he went further to serve the customer.

W7 with the DSD notifications feels like the stronger example though — there's a real conflict between what the org wanted (five notification types to maximize feature delivery) and what actually worked for associates (fewer, more targeted notifications). Anshul talked to the actual users, discovered that more notifications just created fatigue, and pushed back to advocate for just two states. That restraint paid off with a 35% improvement in replenishment — proving the customer need was better served by doing less, not more. Anshul talked to associates in the field and discovered that only two of the five planned notification states—ENROUTE and ARRIVED—were actually actionable; the others either came too early or too late. He pushed back on the product spec to notify only on those two states, avoiding notification fatigue that would make associates ignore alerts entirely. This decision to prioritize what customers could actually act on over comprehensive coverage resulted in a 35% improvement in replenishment efficiency. I'm realizing this maps to Customer Obsession as the core leadership principle, even though the recruiter mentioned Learn and Be Curious and Bias for Action. Now I'm structuring the full story: the situation at Walmart where our team managed supplier data APIs for companies like Pepsi and Coca-Cola, and the specific DSD problem where associates weren't getting timely notifications about deliveries arriving at store docks.

I was tasked with building a mobile push notification system to alert associates when DSD deliveries happened, integrating with the supplier events API and Walmart's mobile push infrastructure. The original spec called for notifications across five lifecycle states, but before diving into implementation, I went directly to the associates themselves rather than relying on product assumptions. They made it clear that only two states actually mattered — when the truck was en route and when it arrived — because those were the only windows where they could take immediate action. The earlier states were too premature and the completed state meant they'd already missed the opportunity.

I pushed back on the spec because sending five notifications per delivery trains people to ignore them entirely. I argued for a more targeted approach: notify only on the two actionable states. I also built a commodity-type mapping through configuration so suppliers show up as readable categories like "Beverages" instead of internal IDs, eliminating the need for redeployment when onboarding new vendors.

The result was a 35% improvement in replenishment timing, measured by how quickly associates scanned inventory after the arrival notification. The system worked because every alert was something they could actually act on.

Now I'm thinking through the technical details and how to explain this in an interview — the specific APIs involved, the state machine design, and how I'd handle the likely pushback questions about access, stakeholder buy-in, and measurement methodology. What I actually found when I talked to the associates was that PLANNED and COMPLETED notifications weren't landing — the truck was still hours away in one case, and the delivery window had already closed in the other. Five pings per delivery just created noise they'd start tuning out. So I went back to the team and made the case for two notifications instead: ENROUTE and ARRIVED, the only moments when they could actually act. They were skeptical at first, but the data backed it up — we saw a 35% improvement in how quickly associates scanned inventory after arrival, which meant the notifications were actually being used instead of ignored.

Now I'm thinking through whether this is the right story to use here, or if there's something better that shows going beyond what the org initially needed. The shared library project comes to mind — the org wanted to decommission Splunk for cost reasons, but what made it interesting was building in BigQuery external tables so suppliers could query their own audit data directly, not just solving the internal cost problem. I'm seeing another strong example here — a major account was stuck in a 2-day debug cycle with 50+ re-queries because the org's system lacked visibility. Anshul built BigQuery external tables with row-level security so suppliers could self-serve debugging, cutting that down to 30 seconds and saving about 12 hours of support work weekly. The org just wanted cost savings, but he prioritized giving customers actual transparency. W7 still feels like the cleaner narrative though because the tension is more explicit — org pushes for one thing, customer needs something different. The key insight is that Anshul didn't remove the data generation—the events were still being created in the backend system, just the notifications were filtered to 2 states. So the org's reporting capabilities stayed intact while the user experience improved based on actual customer feedback. This reframes W7 as the product team's initial plan to surface all states versus Anshul's research-backed recommendation to simplify the notification layer, which is a meaningful tension between feature completeness and user-centric design. was on the Data Ventures team building supplier-facing APIs, and we were working on a Direct Store Delivery notification system. The core problem: store associates only discovered supplier deliveries during periodic check rounds every 2–4 hours, so goods sat at docks unshelved. My task was to wire DSD lifecycle events (PLANNED, STARTED, ENROUTE, ARRIVED, COMPLETED) into Walmart's push notification system to alert the right associates at the right stores. Before diving into code, I insisted on talking to the associates themselves—

not relying on product assumptions. What I learned was critical: PLANNED and STARTED were too early since trucks could be hours away, COMPLETED was useless since the delivery already happened, and notifying on all five states would just train people to ignore the system. So I pushed back on the spec and argued we should only notify on ENROUTE and ARRIVED—two actionable states per delivery. The team's instinct was that felt like less coverage, but I walked them through the associate feedback and the notification fatigue risk to make the case.

We aligned on two states. I also built out the commodity-type mapping so associates see readable categories instead of vendor IDs, and made it configurable so adding new suppliers doesn't require a code deploy.

The result was a 35% improvement in replenishment timing across 1,200+ associates in 300+ stores—measured by how quickly inventory got scanned after the ARRIVED notification. The system worked because every ping was something they could actually act on.

On the technical side, the notification filter lives in our service layer and uses Walmart's internal push API to target only the right store's receiving team. The commodity mappings are stored in our config service so updates don't need a redeploy. The core insight was that notification fatigue is real—if alerts aren't consistently actionable, people stop paying attention, which is why we cut it down to two states instead of five. So this was late 2024, building a notification system for Direct Store Delivery where suppliers drop goods at store docks but associates weren't finding out until hours later during their rounds. The product spec wanted notifications on all five lifecycle states, but I pushed back and actually talked to associates on the dock first — they told me planned was too early, completed was too late, and five pings per delivery just creates notification fatigue. I argued we should only notify on en route and arrived, the two moments where they could actually act on the information right then.

The team's initial reaction was that this felt like less coverage, so I walked them through what the associates had actually said. We aligned on the narrower approach. The result was a 35% improvement in replenishment timing across 1,200+ associates and 300+ stores — measured as the gap between the arrived event and when they first scanned inventory. The notifications worked because people didn't tune them out.

Now I'm doing a self-check on the language I used — scanning for any corporate jargon that might've crept in. Actually, I need to think through what the organization's competing need really was here. The tension seems to be between the product team's initial plan for five notification states versus the associates' need for just two actionable ones. The org might have been trying to deliver the full feature spec, or maybe the suppliers wanted comprehensive tracking across all states. Let me figure out which customer need was actually being prioritized over the org's need. The key difference is that W2 shows Anshul prioritizing supplier self-service debugging over the org's simpler cost-cutting goal—building external tables with row-level security so they could diagnose failures in 30 seconds instead of waiting days. W7 has a more explicit conflict with product specs and stronger customer research, but W2 demonstrates the actual trade-off between organizational efficiency and customer empowerment more clearly.

I'll go with W7 since it has the cleaner narrative arc: the org wanted all five states, customer research revealed only two were needed, Anshul pushed back on the spec, and the outcome validated that customer-first approach.

blocks.md:
## "Tell me about a time you prioritized customer needs over your org's needs" — Amazon Customer Obsession

**Story: W7 — DSD supplier notifications · Customer Obsession · ~100s**

> 💬 This question maps to **Customer Obsession** — not one of the two LPs your recruiter flagged, but standard Amazon behavioral interview. You'll almost certainly get it. Frame the whole answer around: *I went to the customer before writing a line of code, and what I found changed the product.*

---

### Situation

Late 2024 at Walmart. I was on the Data Ventures team — supplier-facing APIs for companies like Pepsi and Coca-Cola. One initiative was a notification system for Direct Store Delivery, or DSD. The way DSD works: suppliers drop goods at store docks, and associates have to go find and shelve them. The problem was associates only found out about deliveries during periodic check rounds — sometimes every 2–4 hours. Goods sat at the dock, shelves went empty, and nobody had visibility.

### Task

My job was to wire the DSD lifecycle events from our platform into Walmart's internal mobile push API and get the right notification to the right store associate in real time.

### Action

The product spec had 5 lifecycle states: PLANNED, STARTED, ENROUTE, ARRIVED, COMPLETED. The ask was to notify on all five.

Before I wrote a line of code, I pushed to actually talk to associates. Not the product team's assumptions about what they'd want — actual people working the dock.

What they told me was pretty clear. PLANNED is too early — the truck is still hours out. STARTED, same. COMPLETED is too late — the delivery already happened. ENROUTE and ARRIVED are the only two where an associate can physically go to the dock and do something right now.

And the bigger risk: if you page someone five times for one delivery, they start ignoring the pings. Notification fatigue is real. Once you've trained people to tune out your system, the whole thing is broken.

So I pushed back on the spec. The org's default was "more states = better coverage." I said: we notify on ENROUTE and ARRIVED only. Every ping has to mean something they can act on.

The team's first instinct was "that feels like less." I walked them through the associate conversations and the fatigue risk. We aligned on two states.

I also made the commodity-type mapping configurable via CCM — so associates see "Beverages" instead of a vendor ID, and onboarding a new supplier doesn't need a code deploy.

### Result

35% improvement in replenishment timing across 1,200+ associates and 300+ stores — measured as the gap between the ARRIVED event timestamp and first inventory scan in the store management system. The notifications worked because every ping was something people could act on. They didn't tune it out.

---

**Technical depth — if they probe:**
- **SUMO V3** — Walmart's internal mobile push service; targeting is by domain + siteId + role, so only the right store's receiving team gets pinged
- **DSD state machine** — PLANNED → STARTED → ENROUTE → ARRIVED → COMPLETED; the cp-nrti-apis service fires Kafka events on each transition; the notification filter sits in our service layer, not in the push API
- **CCM** — Walmart's centralized config management; commodity mappings (vendor ID → label) live there, so a new supplier onboards with a config change, no redeploy
- **Notification fatigue** — the core engineering argument: if less than ~70% of pings require action, users start ignoring the channel; ENROUTE and ARRIVED are both ~100% actionable

> 💬 If they ask "what is notification fatigue?" just say: "If I ping you for things you can't act on, eventually you start ignoring all my pings — even the ones that matter. It's like a car alarm nobody responds to anymore."

**Likely follow-ups:**
1. *How did you get access to associates to talk to?* → Worked through the product team and store-ops contacts; joined a store visit.
2. *What if a supplier wanted tracking across all 5 states?* → Those events were still fired and available via the data APIs — the question was only which ones triggered a push notification.
3. *How did you measure the 35%?* → The business team tracked it on their operational dashboard; not my instrumentation. Gap between ARRIVED event and first scan in their store management system.
4. *What did the product team say when you pushed back?* → First reaction was "that's less coverage." I walked through the associate feedback and the fatigue argument. Got alignment pretty quickly.
5. *What if ENROUTE/ARRIVED turned out to be wrong?* → That's why we built the CCM config — we could add a state in hours without a deploy if we were wrong.

**What NOT to say:**
- Don't frame it as "the org was wrong" — say you did the research that *changed the direction*
- Don't claim you talked to all 1,200 associates — you talked to a sample through the store visit
- Don't overstate the conflict — this wasn't a fight, it was a product conversation you reframed with evidence from actual users

---

🎙️ **Say-it script:**

OK so this was late 2024. We were building a notification system for Direct Store Delivery — suppliers like Pepsi drop goods at store docks, but associates were only finding out during their check rounds, sometimes hours later. Shelves going empty.

The product spec had 5 lifecycle states — planned, started, en route, arrived, completed — and the ask was to push notifications on all five.

Before I wrote anything, I pushed to actually talk to associates. Not just read a requirements doc — actual people working the dock.

What they told me: planned is too early, the truck's hours away. Completed is too late. And if you ping them five times per delivery, they start ignoring the whole channel. Notification fatigue.

So I pushed back on the spec. I said: we notify on en route and arrived only. Two states. Every ping is something they can act on right now. The team's first instinct was "that's less coverage." I walked through what associates actually said and they came around.

Result was 35% improvement in replenishment timing — the gap between the arrived event and first inventory scan dropped by about a third. 1,200 associates, 300 stores. It worked because people didn't tune it out.

---

💬 **60-second version:** "We were building DSD delivery notifications. Original spec was 5 lifecycle states. I talked to actual associates first — found only en route and arrived were actionable. Pushed back, shipped with two states. 35% faster replenishment, because every ping was something they could do something about."

transcript.text: Tell about a time when you took ownership, went outside of your responsibility and completed a task

transcript.thinking:
The user is asking about a time when Anshul took ownership, went outside of his responsibility, and completed a task. This maps to Amazon's **Ownership** Leadership Principle — "Leaders are owners. They think long term and don't sacrifice long-term value for short-term results. They act on behalf of the entire company, beyond just their own team. They never say 'that's not my job.'"

The target company is Amazon (SDE-3), and the recruiter highlighted **Learn and Be Curious** and **Bias for Action** as the two LPs to focus on. But this question clearly maps to **Ownership**, so I should name that LP.
Now I'm evaluating which story best demonstrates going beyond assigned scope. The Spring Boot migration shows initiative since he volunteered, but it was still core backend work. The Kafka debugging story is stronger — he spent five days investigating a pipeline failure that others weren't digging into, then went further by adding chaos tests, metrics, and alerting infrastructure. That's clearly ownership beyond the immediate fix. I'm still considering the third option to see if it's even more compelling. The W2 shared library stands out most — he wasn't asked to solve other teams' problems, but he recognized the duplication across three services and built the solution anyway, cutting their integration time from weeks to a day. W5 also shows initiative since he volunteered for the Spring Boot migration without being assigned to it. The W5 story has that volunteering angle, and W1 shows how he went beyond just fixing the bug—he built comprehensive monitoring with Micrometer, Grafana alerts, and chaos tests because he understood what real observability meant. But W2 still feels strongest since the "outside responsibility" is clearest: he was tasked with audit logging for his service, then recognized the pattern across teams and built a platform solution that cut costs dramatically. Though I should check if W2 would overlap with what I already covered.

For the answer structure, I'll frame W2 around the Ownership principle—he noticed three teams duplicating the same audit-logging work, proposed building a reusable Spring Boot starter instead of a one-off solution, and handled the technical complexity like thread-pool sizing, idempotent Kafka producers, and Avro serialization to make it production-ready for all teams.

The impact was significant: teams went from two weeks of integration time down to one day, slashed audit infrastructure costs from $50K/month to $500/month, and the library scaled to handle 2M+ events daily with P99 latency under 5ms. It eventually grew beyond the initial three teams to 12+ across the organization. I discovered three other product teams were each building their own audit logging implementations from scratch — same pattern repeated four times over. That duplication meant four separate codebases to maintain, four places where bugs would need fixing, and four migrations when Splunk got decommissioned. I pitched my lead on building a shared Spring Boot starter library instead, something any team could adopt with just a Maven dependency. He signed off, and I spent five weeks designing a servlet filter that would auto-wire through Spring's configuration to intercept requests across all services.

The implementation caches request bodies safely, publishes audit events asynchronously so there's zero latency impact on the actual API calls, and routes everything through Kafka with Avro serialization into BigQuery. I also wrote comprehensive documentation and runbooks so teams could actually adopt it without needing me to answer questions for weeks.

The payoff was significant — teams went from two weeks of integration work down to a single day, and the org cut its Splunk spend from $50K monthly down to about $500 by replacing it with this custom pipeline. The system now handles over 2 million events daily with P99 latencies under 5 milliseconds, and it's been adopted by more teams since launch.

Spending those extra five weeks outside my normal scope meant that every team onboarding after that got a one-line dependency instead of a two-week integration project.

W2 is the stronger story here—the "outside responsibility" angle is more dramatic since it crosses team boundaries entirely, the impact is bigger with that cost reduction and cross-team adoption, and it has a cleaner narrative arc of noticing a problem, proposing a solution, and building it.

I'll use W5 as my backup if they ask for another example, since it also fits the theme well: volunteering to lead the Spring Boot 3 migration despite being a backend engineer focused on features, stepping up because nobody else was addressing the CVEs.

Now I'm ready to write out the clean answer for the interview response. I'm trimming this down to fit the tighter word count — cutting the redundant details about Splunk and the proposal process, keeping just the core impact metrics.

blocks.md:
## "Tell me about a time you took ownership and went outside your responsibility" — Amazon Ownership

**Story: W2 — Shared audit-logging library (`dv-api-common-libraries`) · Ownership · ~110s**

> 💬 This maps to **Ownership** — "never say that's not my job." Even though the recruiter flagged Learn and Be Curious and Bias for Action, Ownership will come up. Frame the whole answer around: *I noticed a problem that wasn't mine to fix, proposed fixing it properly, and ate the extra work.*

---

### Situation

Early 2025 at Walmart. I was building the audit logging pipeline for our main supplier API — `cp-nrti-apis`. The job was to capture every request and response so suppliers like Pepsi could see why their API calls succeeded or failed. One service. My scope.

### Task

Build the audit layer for my team's service. That was the assignment. Nothing more.

### Action

While I was mapping out the design, I went to look at how other teams on the platform were handling the same problem. Three separate product teams, each maintaining their own version of the same pattern — intercept the request, log it, ship it somewhere. Same code, written three times.

That felt wrong. And Splunk — where all four of us would have been logging — was being decommissioned company-wide anyway. So if I built a fourth version, the org would have four separate implementations to migrate, four places for the same bugs to appear.

I wasn't asked to fix that. I went to my lead and said: let me build this once as a shared Spring Boot starter that all three teams can adopt. One Maven dependency, no boilerplate, done. He agreed.

Five weeks of design-to-prod. The core: a servlet filter auto-configured via `spring.factories` — any service just adds the dependency and it wires itself in. The filter intercepts the request, caches the body with `ContentCachingWrapper`, and fires the audit payload asynchronously, fire-and-forget, so it adds zero latency to the actual API call. Thread-pool sized at 6/10/100. Idempotent Kafka producer with Avro serialization. Three-tier pipeline downstream: Kafka → Kafka Connect GCS sink with US/CA/MX routing → BigQuery external tables with row-level security on `supplier_id`, so suppliers could query their own audit data directly without calling our support team.

I also wrote the integration guide and a runbook covering the top failure modes. Without that, teams would have been pinging me with questions for a week anyway.

### Result

Each team went from roughly two weeks of integration effort to one day. Replaced Splunk audit logging — the org was spending around $50K a month — with a custom pipeline at about $500 a month. About 99% cost reduction. Running 2M+ events a day now at P99 under 5ms. It's since been adopted by more teams on the platform beyond those original three.

Going outside my lane cost about five extra weeks. Every team that onboarded after that was one day, not two weeks.

---

**Technical depth — if they probe:**
- **`spring.factories` auto-config** — the starter registers itself via Spring's `AutoConfiguration` mechanism; teams don't write `@Bean` definitions, it just works on dependency add
- **`ContentCachingWrapper`** — Spring's built-in wrapper that buffers the `HttpServletRequest` body so it can be read twice (once for the API, once for audit); without this, the stream is consumed and gone
- **Fire-and-forget async** — the audit call is on a separate thread pool so even a slow Kafka publish can't block the API response; the trade-off is best-effort, not guaranteed delivery in the same transaction
- **Idempotent Kafka producer** — `enable.idempotence=true` + `acks=all`; prevents duplicate audit records during producer retries
- **BigQuery external tables** — point BigQuery at GCS Parquet files; no data copy, no ETL job, suppliers query directly; row-level security via a policy tag on `supplier_id` so Pepsi can't see Unilever's data

> 💬 If they ask about the fire-and-forget trade-off in plain English: "The audit logging can't slow down the actual API — so I run it on a separate thread pool. The risk is if the JVM crashes mid-flight, that one audit record is lost. That's an acceptable trade-off for an audit trail, not for financial transactions."

**Likely follow-ups:**
1. *Why didn't you just assign this to the other teams to build themselves?* → Each would have built it differently; three bugs instead of one; and the Splunk migration would have hit each separately anyway.
2. *What if your lead had said no?* → I would have still built the correct version for my service and documented it well enough that adoption was easy — but the shared-starter pitch got a yes quickly because the cost argument was clear.
3. *How did you get the three other teams to actually adopt it?* → Integration time of one day versus two weeks was the pitch. I also sat with each team for the first adoption to make sure the runbook was complete.
4. *How did you handle it if their audit requirements differed?* → The filter is configurable — which headers to capture, which paths to exclude, payload size limits — all via Spring properties so teams don't fork the code.
5. *Give me another example of going outside your lane.* → *[use W5 below]*

**BACKUP story (if asked for another example): W5 — Spring Boot migration**
Nobody assigned the Spring Boot 2.7 → 3.2 upgrade to me. CVEs were piling up, the security audit was three months out, and it needed to happen. I raised my hand and led it — 158 files, `javax` → `jakarta`, Flagger canary rollout, zero customer impact. That was a platform-level concern I picked up because it needed an owner.

**What NOT to say:**
- Don't say "I took initiative" — that's vague. Say what you specifically noticed and proposed.
- Don't claim the $50K/mo Splunk cost was your discovery alone — the org was already planning to decommission it; you built the replacement that served both internal and supplier needs.
- Don't make it sound like the other teams were doing bad work — they were solving their problem; you just saw a pattern worth consolidating.

---

🎙️ **Say-it script:**

So early 2025. My job was to build audit logging for our main supplier API — one service, my scope.

While mapping out the design, I checked what other teams on the platform were doing for the same thing. Three separate product teams, each maintaining their own version of the same pattern. Intercept the request, log it, ship it somewhere. Three separate implementations.

That felt wrong. And Splunk — where they were all logging — was being decommissioned company-wide anyway. So I went to my lead and said: let me build this once as a shared Spring Boot starter. One Maven dependency, zero setup. Got a yes.

Wasn't assigned to me. I just proposed it.

Five weeks. Auto-configured via `spring.factories` so any service just adds the dependency and it wires itself in. Servlet filter caches the request body, fires the audit payload async — fire-and-forget, so it adds zero latency to the API. Idempotent Kafka producer, Avro serialization, three-tier pipeline into BigQuery so suppliers could query their own audit data directly.

Each team went from two weeks of integration to one day. Replaced Splunk at about $50K a month with a custom pipeline at $500. Running 2 million events a day now, P99 under 5ms.

---

💬 **60-second version:** "I was building audit logging for my service when I noticed three other teams all maintaining the same pattern separately. Wasn't my job to fix that — but I proposed building it once as a shared Spring Boot starter. Five weeks. Result: integration time two weeks → one day per team, Splunk cost $50K/mo → $500/mo, 2M events a day running at P99 under 5ms."

I'm organizing this so it's easy to reference during prep—structured by company and role, with the leadership principles mapped to each story and impact metrics highlighted. Keeping it concise since this is just a working document, not the full narrative.

blocks.md:
Yeah, good call. Let me show you exactly what I have mapped — so when a question lands, I can pick the right story in one second.

---

## Your Story Map — Quick Reference

### Career Arc (3 stops)
| Company | Role | When | What you owned |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PayU** | Intern → SE-I | Jan 2022 – Feb 2023 | Loan disbursal APIs, partner reliability |
| **Good Creator Co.** | SE-I | Feb 2023 – May 2024 | Sole architect of 6 production services (Go + Python) |
| **Walmart** | SE-III | Jun 2024 – present | Supplier-facing data APIs (Spring Boot, Kafka, BigQuery) |

---

### WALMART Stories (W-series)

| Tag | Story | Key numbers | Best LP fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **W1** | 5-day silent Kafka failure debug | Zero data loss after replay; 2-tier lag alert | Dive Deep |
| **W2** | Shared audit-logging library | $50K/mo → $500/mo; 2 weeks → 1 day integration; 2M events/day | Ownership, Deliver Results |
| **W3** | DiscardPolicy feedback — defended, then changed | Zero drops over 90 days | Earn Trust, Have Backbone |
| **W5** | Spring Boot 2.7→3.2 migration (`.block()` call) | 4 weeks not 3 months; 158 files; zero customer impact | **Bias for Action** ← recruiter LP |
| **W7** | DSD notifications (2 states not 5) | 35% replenishment improvement; 1,200 associates, 300 stores | Customer Obsession |
| **W8** | DC Inventory Search API (design-first) | 898-line spec; p99 3s→800ms; 30% faster consumer integration | Ownership |
| **W9** | Cosmos DB → PostgreSQL migration | Zero-downtime; cursor pagination defended under pushback | Are Right a Lot, Have Backbone |
| **W10** | Observability platform | 99.9% SLA upheld; Dynatrace + Prometheus + Grafana + Flagger | Dive Deep |
| **W12** | Why leaving Walmart | framing only — bigger scope, not running away | N/A |

---

### GOOD CREATOR CO. Stories (G-series)

| Tag | Story | Key numbers | Best LP fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **G1** | ClickHouse migration ← **HERO STORY** | 33× throughput; 30s→12s queries; 5× compression; 30% cost cut | Deliver Results, Invent & Simplify |
